title: Expansion on Personal and Professional Level: Annual Planning Framework
summary: Erin walked participants through her integrated personal and professional planning framework, moving from a 30-year vision down to daily standards across three buckets: Energy, Work/Service, and Love/Family. The session then opened into live Q&A covering productivity habits, journaling, dealing with difficult clients over material defects, vetting clients before projects, and navigating construction documentation and cashflow tracking.

teachings.explanation: Opening yourself to expansive, long-horizon thinking removes the constraint of near-term realism and allows you to set audacious goals across all areas of life. The purpose is not to achieve everything in 30 years but to give your mind permission to dream big before working backward to what is actionable now.
teachings.quote: A lot of times we go for massive goals and we hit them, but sometimes it takes longer. So just remember that.

teachings.concept: Three Life Buckets: Energy, Work/Service, Love and Family
teachings.explanation: Everything in life can be organized into three categories: Energy (how you fuel yourself to show up for others), Work and Service (your professional contribution and how you serve), and Love and Family (everyone you love and your relationships). Keeping all three aligned prevents one from overwhelming the others and ensures a sustainable, whole life.
teachings.quote: There is a way to tie this together. When one thing overrides the other, right, when it's all business or it's all family, that's where people can get lost.

teachings.explanation: After setting a one-year goal, the most critical question to ask is 'What needs to be true for this to happen?' This question removes emotional attachment to outcomes and focuses energy on the specific conditions and actions required to make the goal a reality. It is meant to be revisited daily.
teachings.quote: What needs to be true for this to happen? I'm not attached to the outcome. I'm not attached to the emotion. What I am committed to is what needs to be true for this to happen.

teachings.concept: Daily Standards Tied to Annual Goals
teachings.explanation: Your daily non-negotiable actions should be directly traceable back to your annual goals and the three life buckets. Rather than managing a long to-do list, identifying the top three things that must happen each day creates a disciplined feedback loop of compounding progress.
teachings.quote: These are standards period. They have to happen on the daily because at some point you get to the end of the year and we got to look at this.

teachings.concept: Morning Routine as the Foundation of Energy
teachings.explanation: A consistent, non-negotiable morning routine is the mechanism for generating the energy needed to serve others well. The specific activities matter less than the consistency and intentionality behind them. The goal is to clear mental noise and arrive ready to serve.
teachings.quote: Whatever it takes to get rid of all the monkeys on your back, clear your head and get ready to spend the rest of your day serving others, that's what needs to happen.

teachings.explanation: When building an annual plan, the reward for hitting goals should be defined at the start, not after. High achievers tend to be grinders who skip celebration, which removes a critical motivational anchor. Identifying the reward in advance connects effort to meaning and fun.
teachings.quote: When I hit X, we're going to go to Paris as a family. When I hit Y, whatever it is for you guys, mark it down because there is a part of this world that wants to be fun.

teachings.concept: Parsing Roles and Responsibility in Client Disputes
teachings.explanation: When a problem arises on a project, the first step is to analytically map who made which decision and when. A builder's role is often transactional in material selection disputes where the designer and homeowner made the choices. Inserting yourself as the hero or problem-solver when you were not the decision-maker creates unnecessary financial and emotional liability.
teachings.quote: Keep it factual. Like when we really go through the facts, those are the facts as I hear them. My role was to obtain the material and install it. My role is 100% done.

teachings.concept: Client Vetting: Spotting Unreasonable People Before Signing
teachings.explanation: After a difficult client experience, it is worth rewinding to the moment you decided to take the project and asking what warning signs were present. Social behavior, personal circumstances, and family dynamics around the decision-maker are all signals worth weighing before committing to a contract.
teachings.quote: When you decided to take the project and this is for all of us to talk about, when you decided to take the project, were there any signs of unreasonable people?

teachings.concept: Goals Must Filter to Your Team
teachings.explanation: Personal and business goals are not just individual exercises. A leader should understand each team member's strengths and ambitions and actively look for alignment between company goals and what each person uniquely brings. This turns goal-setting into a team-building and retention tool.
teachings.quote: I have every one of them listed here and I'm constantly trying to find how we can support, like yes they do this job here, but what else, what's the other, what's their secret sauce? And how can we align with that?

actionable_advice: Start with a 30-year vision list across all areas of life before narrowing to one-year goals, and allow yourself to think without realistic constraints.
Organize all goals and daily actions into three buckets: Energy, Work and Service, and Love and Family.
After setting any annual goal, write down the answer to 'What needs to be true for this to happen?' and revisit it daily.
Identify and commit to your top three must-complete tasks each morning, not five or ten, just three that must happen before you sleep.
Define rewards for hitting annual goals at the beginning of the year, not after, and tie them explicitly to specific milestones.
Review your annual goals at least once daily, Erin recommends three times a day, by keeping them accessible on your phone.
In material or scope disputes with clients, map out factually who made each decision and limit your role statement to what is actually true about your scope.
When evaluating a difficult past client, extract one or two specific early warning signs you can watch for in future client vetting.
Create SOPs by recording a Loom or Zoom video of yourself explaining a process, then transcribe it and use an AI tool like Gemini to format it as a written SOP.
Ask yourself regularly: who do I need to know that I don't yet know? Treat it as a genuine question with an open answer.
client_challenges: A participant (Janine) had a wallpaper installation where the manufacturer refused to acknowledge a defect on the primary bedroom paper, leaving the homeowner unhappy and the designer unresponsive, and was unsure how to resolve it and whether she bore any financial responsibility.

A participant (Marla) described a client who brought in an outside vendor without the builder's knowledge, damaged the stone, and then demanded the GC fix and pay for it, ultimately locking out the builder, withholding payment to subs, and forcing a legal walkaway.

A participant (Renee) was confused about how to correctly input subcontractor and material costs into the cashflow template, resulting in her company profit appearing inflated because outgoing costs were not being separated from incoming revenue.
A participant (Connor) was struggling with how to translate designer selection documents, which vary widely in format and detail, into a consistent internal scope and bidding system.
A participant (Julie) asked how to maintain productivity throughout the week so she could look back at the end of Friday and know concretely what was accomplished and what was deferred.
A participant (Martha) requested the detailed build schedule template referencing a construction sequencing tool from Erin's David Weekley Homes experience.
Julie requested SOP templates and roles and responsibilities documentation formatted for easy use in hiring and onboarding.
notable_quotes: On daily goal review: 'My cadence for annual goals is three times a day. I just read it. I just stay in the game, stay in it, stay in it, stay in it.'
On client dispute resolution: 'Not everything requires an opinion, even though we're asked. It's okay not to have an opinion and not everything has to have a resolution.'

On avoiding self-serving goal-setting: 'When there's so much focus on hitting these things, it turns into a weird thing where like, it's about me. And it's really not. Like we have to recognize, no, this is not about me, me, me. This is really about what can I give, what can I share.'

On the compounding effect of daily journaling: 'You find yourself in a position you never thought you'd be in. Because of all this stuff. I had no idea that was going to happen, but because of all this stuff.'
On burning the boats: 'It was God said go. So I went.' — Julie, on quitting her tech job to enter residential construction.
On morning routine discipline: 'I've been at the gym at one thirty in the morning to make it happen. It doesn't matter. It is not skipped.'
